issues.current_text: a `GroupedIterator` producing `Seq[B]`s of size `size`, except the last segment (which may be the only segment) will be truncated if there are fewer than `size` elements remaining to be grouped. This behavior can be configured.
issues.problem: The trailing 'This behavior can be configured.' is vague — it does not say *how* to configure it. Referencing `withPartial` and `withPadding` (already mentioned in the method's example block) would make the pointer actionable.
issues.suggestion: a `GroupedIterator` producing `Seq[B]`s of size `size`, except the last segment (which may be the only segment) will be truncated if there are fewer than `size` elements remaining to be grouped. The truncation behavior can be overridden via `withPartial` or `withPadding`.

issues.current_text: an iterator that produces the elements of this iterator with the elements of `patchElems` inserted at position `from`, replacing `replaced` original elements
issues.problem: Minor wording: 'inserted ... replacing' is slightly contradictory — `patch` replaces a range and substitutes the patch elements there. Saying 'spliced in at position `from`' or 'substituted at position `from`' reads more cleanly. The main scaladoc already covers the edge cases (negative `from`, oversized `replaced`), so no need to repeat them here.
issues.suggestion: an iterator that yields the elements of this iterator with `replaced` elements starting at position `from` substituted by the elements of `patchElems`

general_feedback: The added @return and @param descriptions are accurate, start lowercase, use backticks consistently, and align with the surrounding scaladoc style. The `span`, `sliceIterator`, `empty`, and `UnfoldIterator` descriptions are especially clear and capture the relevant edge cases (negative `until`, `NoSuchElementException` on empty, the `Some`/`None` sentinel for unfold). Only two minor suggestions: the `grouped` @return ends with a vague configurability note that could name the configuration hooks, and the `patch` @return phrasing mixes 'inserted' with 'replacing'.
